name: Cova des Corral des Porcs
best_season: Avoid summer due to high CO2 concentration
access: From Palma, take the train from Estación Intermodal
description: A LIC-protected karstic cave featuring multiple chambers connected by narrow passages, with notable geological features including a fault mirror, coraloid speleothems, dissolution channels, and eccentric stalactites. CO2 levels can reach 2.7-5%, making summer visits inadvisable. The cave shows significant subsidence processes throughout.
full_description:
IMPORTANT

The Cova de sa Guitarreta is catalogued as a Site of Community Importance (LIC). These sites must be protected, preserved, recovered, and restored. On the island of Mallorca, there are a total of 30 cavities catalogued as LIC. Currently (see date on the cave's information sheet), none of these sites can be accessed without the mandatory authorization from the Ministry of the Environment and Territory, so before making any visit, the required permit must be obtained to enter.

DESCRIPTION OF THE CAVITY

Entrance Zone I

A small vestibule of 5 x 4 m depth ends in a window of reduced dimensions through which one must lie down to cross by crawling, connecting to a 9 m long corridor with a low ceiling that leads into a chamber of larger dimensions, where a reasonable quantity of formations are present. To the north of the chamber, in a considerable descent, one goes down to a sort of funnel-shaped ramp that leads into a difficult area to traverse due to the scarcity of space, finally opening into the Sala de s'Entrada II (Entrance Chamber II).

Sala de s'Entrada II (Entrance Chamber II)

To access the Sala de s'Entrada, one must first crawl a few meters until finding a window that, after an easy downclimb, places us inside. It is a structural chamber of 10 x 9 x 4 m with a teepee-shaped ceiling morphology, formed by the subsidence process that occurred when the subsurface was emptied. The chamber is not devoid of formations; in fact, it is reasonably decorated, although it appears to lack activity. On the NE side, a small opening connects to the Entrance Zone I, while to the north, the cave continues.

Es Racó (The Corner)

This is a small corner of 3.5 x 5 m, hidden behind a gour in the Sala de s'Entrada II. The reduced dimensions of the entrance between two small columns mean that one must even remove their helmet to access the interior of this small corner. Number 11 on the topography.

Pou des Caos (Chaos Shaft)

A window situated at the head (SE zone of the chamber) allows descent into a ten-meter-deep shaft with two levels. On the first of the two levels encountered during descent, only the existence of some formations is worth noting. On the lower level, the enormous collapse suffered as a result of the subsidence process that occurred here, which may have also affected the Sala de s'Entrada II, is clearly visible. The large rock blocks pile up against each other while leaving spaces between them, giving a certain sense of insecurity in the place.

Sala des Mirall de Falla (Fault Mirror Chamber)

This is one of the largest chambers in the cavity and also houses a significant number of speleothems. Perhaps the most significant feature of this place is the subsidence process that undoubtedly was related to the Pou des Caos, in which a detachment caused a beautiful fault mirror on the eastern side of the chamber.

Galeria sense Nom (Unnamed Gallery)

On the SW side of the Sala des Mirall de Falla, a gallery opens behind a barrier formed by small columns, of little interest. It follows the same direction as the rest of the fracture, eventually becoming impassable.

Galeria des Paleonivells (Paleolevels Gallery)

Following the NW-SE direction like the rest of the cave's main fracture, this gallery is accessed by climbing very carefully from the Sala de sa Cascada (Waterfall Chamber). The most noteworthy feature of the place is what appear to be ancient paleolevels marked on a block that has been moved from its original position, which may have been part of an ancient deposit where water level oscillations inside left their mark.

Pou des Coraloides (Coraloid Shaft)

With a maximum depth of eight meters, it contains a 16 m passable level following the same orientation as the rest of the cave's fracture, with variable height. Inside, and hence its name, large quantities of coraloid speleothems predominate, among which the botryoidal variety is abundant, covering a large part of the walls.

Sala de sa Cascada (Waterfall Chamber)

With a width of 6 m, a length of 20 m, and a height of 9 m, it is the largest chamber in the cavity.

Galeria des CO2 (CO2 Gallery)

Entering this small gallery of 3 x 7.5 x 2 m through the reduced entrance involves making contact with mud that will accompany us from this point until reaching the Sala de ses Excèntriques (Chamber of the Eccentrics). It is in this zone of the cave where measurements taken to check the amount of carbon dioxide (CO2) present reached a concentration of 2.70%. In measurements carried out in previous years, the CO2 concentration reached 5%.

Galeria de sa Corrosió (Corrosion Gallery)

It begins right at the eastern zone of the Sala de ses Excèntriques, in the approximately four-meter-deep shaft, through a lamination passage of considerable slope through which the suffering speleologist must travel, dealing not only with the lack of space but also with water and mud. Throughout the entire route, the gallery environment is affected by corrosion processes, with abundant speleothems present.

Sala de ses Excèntriques (Chamber of the Eccentrics)

A beautiful small chamber of 11 x 18 x 2 m. In it, a significant stratigraphic detachment is visible, which is currently part of the ceiling of the Galeria de sa Corrosió. Many speleothems are housed within, notably a column of approximately one meter in diameter covered with eccentrics for the most part, as well as "curtains" of delicate formations that separate this chamber from the Sala des Canals de Dissolució.

Sala des Canals de Dissolució (Dissolution Channels Chamber)

Measuring 12 x 9 x 3 m, access to this chamber from the Sala de ses Excèntriques is through a small barrier of formations, among which multiple eccentric stalactites are found, via a small window. Traversing some parts of it generates certain difficulty. Perhaps the most significant feature is in the ceiling zone, where dissolution channels can be contemplated through which pressurized water was responsible for the formation of this zone of the cavity.
